content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COrVIMISSION
P.O. Box 807
. 428 J Street
. Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
. Fax (916) 322-0886
January 28, 1999
Bill Daniels
Director
Sacramento County Fire Protection District
8401 Cranford Way
Citrus Heights, California 95610
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 1-98-306
Dear Mr. Daniels:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions ofthe
Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’ You have not identified a specific decision, therefore, we
consider your letter to be one for informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18329(c).
2
QUESTION
If another fire board director voted on an issue that was detennined to be a conflict of
interest for that director, could you be fined for that official’s action?
CONCLUSION
The conflict-of-interest provisions impose liability only on a public official for making,
participating in, or attempting to influence a decision in which the public official has a financial
interest. You do not have a conflict of interest if another director who has a conflict of interest
participates in a decision. In addition, merely participating in the decision would not subject you
to liability for aiding and abetting under the Act.
Government Code sections 81000
- 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)


File No. 1-98-306
Page 2
FACTS
You are a director for the Sacramento County Fire Protection District (the “District”).
You have not presented any facts regarding a specific decision, or the manner in which another
director has a conflict of interest in any decision before the District.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
attempting to influence a governmental decision, in which the official has a financial interest.
3
This provision prohibits you from participating in a decision before the District if you should
have a financial interest in that decision. However, this provision does not prevent you from
making or participating in a decision as a result of another director’s conflict. Thus, you would
not have a conflict of interest if a director who has a conflict of interest participates in that
decision. In addition, in the Leahy Advice Letter, No. A-95-038, we discussed that your mere
participation in the decision would not constitute aiding and abetting under the Act.
4
(Leahy
Advice Letter, No. A-95-038, Kloeker Advice Letter, No. A-95-01 8, copies enclosed.)
Since you have not provided any facts regarding the decision, we cannot offer any further
analysis. We have enclosed general information regarding this issue.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
B
Amy Bisson Holloway
Staff Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:ABH:tls
Regulations 18702.1
- 18702.3 defme when an official makes, participates in making, or attempts to use
his or her official position to influence a governmental decision.
Section 83116.5 provides that any person who purposely or negligently causes any other person to
violate any provision of this title, or who aids and abets any other person in the violation of any provision of the
Act, shall be liable for administrative penalties under the Act.

QUESTION: If another fire board director voted on an issue that was detennined to be a conflict of
interest for that director, could you be fined for that official’s action?

CONCLUSION: The conflict-of-interest provisions impose liability only on a public official for making,
participating in, or attempting to influence a decision in which the public official has a financial
interest. You do not have a conflict of interest if another director who has a conflict of interest
participates in a decision. In addition, merely participating in the decision would not subject you
to liability for aiding and abetting under the Act.2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal
